title: What an American thinks about Moldova

transcript.text: This video is what an American thinks about Moldova.
I spent, I took the train to Moldova from Romania, from right near the border of Romania.
And I spent one afternoon there, but I slept because I was really tired.
And then the entire next day, and then I took the night train out of there.
So I spent pretty much only one day in the capital of Romania, Cisinau.
I hung out one night when I was there with my two roommates in the hostel, this Belgian guy and an American girl.
And we, the American girl said that she had read in some like happiness index that the Romanians got last place in all the countries of the whole world.
They were the least happy people.
So, and, um, looking around, walking around Romania, they, yeah, they did seem kind of like the, I mean, Moldova.
They did seem kind of like the Romanians and personality wise, just kind of like, go on.
Um, we had one waiter at some bar, though, who was like really happy and cheerful in vain.
Um, I don't think they could speak English.
I had trouble speaking English with any other people there.
The people at our hostel, it was interesting.
They had, um, it was these two old people.
Neither of them could speak English at all.
But she was really friendly.
She wanted to make sure that my bike was safe.
You know, didn't want me to lock my bike out outside.
So she let me keep bringing it in, even though there was hardly enough room.
But, um, I talked for a long time, actually, a couple hours with this really talkative Moldovan girl,
My roommate in the hostel in Lucharest.
And she was telling me about Romania.
Romania, I mean Moldova.
Moldova is part of Romania.
They're all Romanians.
And it's, Moldova is a different country because the Russians went in there and snatched it.
And then they owned it.
And then with the breakup of the USSR, um, it became its own country.
Um, instead of putting it back in with Romania, since it was so, it got so Russianized,
They just made it Moldova.
Um, and I guess it's, it's an, it's, you know, it's, it's a distinct part of Romania.
It's always been, you know, the, a certain part of Romania, like a certain dialect, I guess,
Of Romanian, but their language is like, is Romanian.
But I guess since they were part of, it was a certain, I can't remember for how long it
Was, but they all had to learn Russian.
So it's a, um, it's a bilingual country, I guess.
If you're older, you speak Russian as a first language.
And then if you're younger, then I guess you'd speak Romanian as a first language.
It's, or everybody's is bilingual.
I was in a, like, and, and I think they were, I guess, I can't remember, but I think they
Used both, um, both, uh, the Latin and the Cyrillic script.
But I saw Cyrillic more.
So they're still, um, I think, mainly Cyrillic.
So, Chisinau is a capital city.
It's, it's not a big city, though.
I guess it's a medium-sized capital city.
It's, it's not nearly as big.
It's not a big city, like most capital cities, like Bucharest.
But it's bigger than some capital cities I've been to, like, um, on this trip.
Like, uh, actually, I guess not.
You have two kinds of capital cities.
You have the big ones and then the ones that aren't so big.
This one wasn't so big.
This was, like, as big as Pristina.
I thought it was bigger than, um, it was bigger than, uh, the capital of, of, uh, of Montenegro.
Um, um, Podgarica.
Podgarica wasn't big at all.
But anyways, um, Chisinau, it had, it had a couple of, um, um, squares in the center of it,
Which were okay, which were pretty.
Uh, I noticed a lot of people sitting around, though, which was interesting, which I haven't
Noticed, that I hadn't noticed so much until then.
People go to the park and they sit on the park benches.
There's, I mean, they do that actually a lot in those countries, like, in Sofia, I noticed
It.
But I didn't really notice people not doing anything.
Until I got to Chisinau, I'd see people just sitting there.
Just sit on the park bench and sit there.
Which is, uh, something you'll see a lot in third world countries, is people just sitting
There.
Um, and you know they're not doing anything, they're just sitting there.
I sat down and there was this one guy sitting on a park bench who I sat down next to because
I bought a beer and I was just drinking it.
And he was talking to me a little bit.
He said his, like, daughter had moved to America.
And, um, but he didn't speak any English, so it was, you know, not much to talk about.
Um, and, uh, they had this lake there that I wanted to look at, but I had to go down these
Big steep stairs and I didn't feel like carrying my bike down itself because I had all my gear
With me.
So I ventured around a little bit more.
I went to this other big park area and, um, on the map it looked pretty, like this big
Park in the center.
But I was riding my bike through it and it was really dilapidated.
And even getting to it, I went through part of this neighborhood of these, you know, those
Big communist, um, apartment buildings and the, it was really dilapidated going there.
The roads going in there were not paved or they were paved, but there were lots of really
Big potholes going up there and the sidewalks were cracked.
It was like, you know, parts of Albania can be like that.
And then I went into the park and it was really dilapidated.
It was like a wilderness area sort of.
And, uh, the, the, uh, paved areas going in were cracked and pothole-y and everything.
There were people in there, but one really bizarre thing, yet again, is you see these
People and they're sitting around and they weren't talking to each other.
They were just sitting there.
Like, so I don't know, maybe Moldovans, they have some, they're just, maybe it's because
Maybe due to something happening with the Russians or something, suppressing them with
The communism just made them not, took their personalities away or something.
Their education wasn't high enough to talk about anything because I saw a lot of that.
But there are guys with their shirts off and their shirts pulled up like this.
They do, they do that a lot in that area to keep, to cool down that I saw.
Um, but there are, anyways, there were lots of people there, um, in that park.
Um, and, um, and, uh, yeah, then I had dinner at some pizza place and the, the waitress was
Really nice there.
But, uh, yeah, that's Moldova.
That's all I really know about.
The girl who I talked to for, the Moldovan girl, she said that she never wanted to go back
To Moldova.
That Moldova's totally screwed, um, and that it's run by gangsters.
And one really weird thing about Moldova that I noticed that was really weird is I wanted
To get a new video camera because I left my video camera on the counter of a bathroom and
Walked away.
Even, and I only had the video camera for like three weeks.
Totally sucked.
And I got the video camera.
It was a, it was a Canon video camera.
Um, and I got it for 200 euros in, uh, in Patras, Greece, which is like 250 bucks.
And so I walked into a store.
I saw the store for electronics.
I walk into the store and I see the same video camera and I do the exchange and it turns
Out it's $550, $564 or something.
So they marked it up big time.
Um, I don't know what the deal with that is, if it's, uh, you know, the taxes they put
On imports for electronics or some kind of monopoly that electronics stores have there.
Um, but that, that sounds, I'm not an economist or anything, but that, to me, that sounds mafioso
To me.
That sounds wrong.
There's no reason why stuff like that should be so expensive in a country where people are
So poor.
That's a huge difference in price.
So, that's what I think about Romania.
Um, when, I mean, Moldova, but since it was Moldova and I speak Romanian, when I talk
To people, I just speak to them in Spanish because they, they can understand Spanish because
It's a romance language.
And even the woman at the train station who was getting agitated with me for no reason.
That's kind of something that the Romanians and Moldovans, I noticed, they're like, ah,
They get like upset.
Kind of like how you would imagine a Russian to be, like a gruff Russian.
But anyway, she was talking to me in, uh, in French.
She's like, you understand French?
I was like, yeah.
So, cause I can understand French too, cause it's a romance language.
So that was interesting.
Um, and then I took the train into, into Kiev, Ukraine.
Okay.
So, that's what an American thinks about Moldova.
Oh, what?
Well, thank you so much.
Thank you.
